Gentamicin injection is available as 40 mg/mL in 2 mL vials. What volume of gentamicin injection (40 mg/mL) should be drawn up?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the normal dose
• Normal dose = Weight × Dose per kg
• 67 kg × 5 mg/kg = 335 mg

Step 2: Check against the maximum single dose
• 335 mg is below the maximum of 500 mg, so no cap is needed

Step 3: Apply renal dose adjustment
• eGFR is 15 mL/min/1.73m², which falls in the 15–29 range
• Adjustment: reduce to 40% of normal dose
• Adjusted dose = 335 mg × 0.40 = 134 mg

Step 4: Calculate the volume to draw up
• Stock concentration: 40 mg/mL
• Volume = Dose ÷ Concentration = 134 mg ÷ 40 mg/mL = 3.35 mL

Answer: 3.35 mL

Why other answers are wrong:
• 13.4 mL — No renal adjustment applied (335 ÷ 25, calculation error)
• 2.23 mL — May have applied a different adjustment factor
• 1.68 mL — Half the correct answer
• 33.5 mL — Decimal error (× 10)
summary: Daily dose = Weight × mg/kg/day = 60 kg × 40 mg/kg/day = 2400 mg/day.
key_points: Patient weight: 60 kg | Dose: 40 mg/kg/day | Calculation: 60 × 40 = 2400 mg/day | Usually divided into 4 doses (QDS)
why_wrong_e: 24000 mg = calculation error (×10)
clinical_pearl: Flucloxacillin is antistaphylococcal penicillin for skin/soft tissue and bone infections. Adult dose typically 250-500mg QDS, but weight-based dosing ensures adequate levels. Hepatotoxicity risk - limit to 14 days when possible.
exam_tip: Daily dose = weight × mg/kg/DAY. Flucloxacillin hepatotoxicity warning - avoid prolonged courses.
related_topics: Flucloxacillin | Antistaphylococcal antibiotics | Daily dose calculations | Hepatotoxicity

question_text: A 72 kg patient with an eGFR of 35 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed gentamicin at 5 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 500 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 30-60 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 60% of normal dose. Gentamicin injection is available as 40 mg/mL in 2 mL vials. What is the correct gentamicin dose for this patient?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the normal dose
• Normal dose = Weight × Dose per kg
• 72 kg × 5 mg/kg = 360 mg

Step 2: Check against the maximum single dose
• 360 mg is below the maximum of 500 mg, so no cap is needed

Step 3: Apply renal dose adjustment
• eGFR is 35 mL/min/1.73m², which falls in the 30–60 range
• Adjustment: reduce to 60% of normal dose
• Adjusted dose = 360 mg × 0.60 = 216 mg

Answer: 216 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 21.6 mg — Decimal error (÷ 10)
• 144.1 mg — May have applied 40% instead of 60%
• 108 mg — This is 30% of the normal dose
• 2160 mg — Decimal error (× 10)
summary: Loading dose = Weight × Dose/kg = 80 kg × 10 mg/kg = 800 mg.
key_points: Patient weight: 80 kg | Loading dose: 10 mg/kg | Calculation: 80 × 10 = 800 mg | IV rate: maximum 50 mg/min
why_wrong_e: 1600 mg = 20 mg/kg (double dose - toxic)
clinical_pearl: Phenytoin loading achieves therapeutic levels rapidly for seizure control. IV phenytoin maximum rate 50 mg/min (or 25 mg/min in elderly) to avoid hypotension and arrhythmias. Fosphenytoin can be given faster (150 mg PE/min).
exam_tip: Phenytoin loading: 10-20 mg/kg IV. Remember max infusion rate 50 mg/min. Monitor ECG and BP during infusion.
related_topics: Antiepileptic loading | Phenytoin pharmacology | Seizure management | IV drug safety

question_text: A 75 kg patient requires adrenaline at 10 micrograms/minute. The infusion is prepared as 5 mg in 50 mL of sodium chloride 0.9% (concentration: 0.1 mg/mL). The maximum infusion rate is 50 mL/hour. Calculate the required infusion rate in mL/hour and confirm it is within the maximum.
explanation: Renal dose adjustment by interval extension: Original interval = 8 hours. Extended by 50% means: New interval = Original × 1.5 = 8 × 1.5 = 12 hours. In renal impairment, aminoglycosides like gentamicin accumulate due to reduced clearance. Extending the dosing interval (rather than reducing dose) maintains peak concentrations for efficacy while allowing trough levels to fall.
summary: Original: 8 hours. Extended by 50%: 8 × 1.5 = 12 hours.
key_points: Original interval: 8 hours | Extension: 50% | New interval: 8 × 1.5 = 12 hours
clinical_pearl: Extending dosing intervals is one strategy for renal impairment. Alternative is dose reduction with same interval.
exam_tip: 50% extension = multiply by 1.5. Interval increases, not dose.
related_topics: Renal dosing | Interval adjustment | Aminoglycosides

question_text: A 74 kg adult patient requires IV maintenance fluids at 30 mL/kg/day. The fluid prescribed is NaCl 0.18%/Glucose 4% with 40 mmol/L KCl added. NaCl 0.18%/Glucose 4% contains 31 mmol/L sodium. How much potassium (mmol) will the patient receive in 24 hours?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total daily fluid volume
• Volume = Weight × mL/kg/day
• Volume = 74 kg × 30 mL/kg/day = 2220 mL/day = 2.22 L/day

Step 2: Calculate the potassium delivered
• KCl concentration in the bag = 40 mmol/L
• Potassium = Volume (L) × Concentration (mmol/L)
• Potassium = 2.22 L × 40 mmol/L = 88.8 mmol ≈ 89 mmol/day

Answer: 89 mmol/day

Why other answers are wrong:
• 44.5 mmol/day — Half the correct answer (may have used 20 mmol/L)
• 267 mmol/day — Triple the correct answer
• 8.9 mmol/day — Decimal error (÷ 10)
• 890 mmol/day — Decimal error (× 10)
summary: Weekly dose = 7.5 mg × 7 days = 52.5 mg... Wait, question says once weekly, so total is 7.5 mg/week.
key_points: Methotrexate: 7.5 mg once weekly | This IS the weekly dose: 7.5 mg | Note: If answer shows 52.5 mg, question may have different wording
why_wrong_a: 7.5 mg if question asks total weekly and dose is already weekly
why_wrong_d: 75 mg would be 10 times the dose
clinical_pearl: Methotrexate for RA/psoriasis is ONCE WEEKLY - daily dosing causes fatal toxicity. Always verify weekly dosing with patients.
exam_tip: Read carefully: 'once weekly' means 7.5 mg IS the weekly dose. If it said 'daily', multiply by 7.
related_topics: Methotrexate safety | Weekly dosing | High-risk medications

question_text: A 98 kg patient with an eGFR of 40 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed amikacin at 15 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 1500 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 30-60 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 60% of normal dose. Amikacin injection is available as 50 mg/mL in 2 mL vials. What is the correct amikacin dose for this patient?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the normal dose
• Normal dose = Weight × Dose per kg
• 98 kg × 15 mg/kg = 1470 mg

Step 2: Check against the maximum single dose
• 1470 mg is below the maximum of 1500 mg, so no cap is needed

Step 3: Apply renal dose adjustment
• eGFR is 40 mL/min/1.73m², which falls in the 30–60 range
• Adjustment: reduce to 60% of normal dose
• Adjusted dose = 1470 mg × 0.60 = 882 mg

Answer: 882 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 88.2 mg — Decimal error (÷ 10)
• 293.7 mg — May have applied 20% instead of 60%
• 3528 mg — Calculation error (possibly × 4)
• 793.8 mg — May have applied 54% instead of 60%
summary: Calculated: 25 mg/kg × 45 kg = 1125 mg. Max: 1000 mg. Give capped dose: 1000 mg.
key_points: Calculated: 25 × 45 = 1125 mg | Maximum dose: 1000 mg | Calculated > max | Give: 1000 mg
clinical_pearl: Adult ibuprofen maximum is typically 2400 mg/day (800 mg TDS). Single doses capped at 400-800 mg depending on indication.
exam_tip: Always compare calculated to maximum. Use lower value.
related_topics: Dose capping | NSAIDs | Maximum doses

question_text: A patient is started on mirtazapine for depression with insomnia using the following titration schedule: 15 mg at night for 14 days; 30 mg at night for 14 days; 45 mg at night for 28 days. Mirtazapine is available as 15 mg tablets. Calculate the total number of 15 mg tablets needed for the primary drug.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total number of 15 mg tablets for each phase
• Phase 1: 15 mg daily for 14 days → 1 tablet/day × 14 = 14 tablets
• Phase 2: 30 mg daily for 14 days → 2 tablets/day × 14 = 28 tablets
• Phase 3: 45 mg daily for 28 days → 3 tablets/day × 28 = 84 tablets

Step 2: Calculate the total tablets
• Total = 14 + 28 + 84 = 126 tablets

Answer: 126 tablets

Why other answers are wrong:
• 504 tablets — Quadruple the correct answer
• 138.6 tablets — Calculation error
• 84.04 tablets — Only counted the final phase
• 189 tablets — 1.5× the correct answer

Key concept: Convert each dose to the number of tablets per day using the available strength, multiply by the number of days in that phase, then sum all phases.
summary: 125 mg phenytoin from 250 mg/5 mL = 2.5 mL. Volume = (Dose ÷ Concentration) × Volume per unit.
key_points: Suspension concentration: 250 mg in 5 mL | Set up proportion: 250 mg/5 mL = 125 mg/x mL | Cross multiply: 250x = 625, so x = 2.5 mL | Alternative: 125 ÷ 250 × 5 = 0.5 × 5 = 2.5 mL
why_wrong_a: 0.2 mL is far too small - would provide only 10 mg
why_wrong_b: 1.7 mL would provide approximately 85 mg - insufficient
why_wrong_e: 25 mL would provide 1250 mg - 10× overdose, dangerous
clinical_pearl: Phenytoin suspension (Epanutin) has non-linear pharmacokinetics - small dose changes can cause large plasma level changes. Always shake well before use and use an accurate oral syringe.
exam_tip: For suspension volumes: Required dose ÷ Available concentration × Volume = Answer. Check your answer is reasonable.
related_topics: Suspension dosing | Phenytoin pharmacokinetics | Volume calculations

question_text: A patient (weight 80 kg, height 160 cm) with a GFR of 60 mL/min is prescribed carboplatin using the Calvert formula: Dose (mg) = target AUC × (GFR + 25). The target AUC is 5 mg/mL·min and the maximum dose is 800 mg per BNF. The dose is diluted in 500 mL glucose 5% and infused over 1 hour(s). Calculate the correct carboplatin dose in mg.
explanation:
Step 1: Apply the Calvert formula
• Dose (mg) = Target AUC × (GFR + 25)
• Dose = 5 mg/mL·min × (60 + 25)
• Dose = 5 × 85 = 425 mg

Step 2: Check against the maximum dose
• 425 mg is below the maximum of 800 mg per BNF, so no cap is needed

Answer: 425 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 382.5 mg — May have used a different GFR or AUC
• 4250 mg — Decimal error (× 10)
• 141.5 mg — Calculation error
• 1275 mg — May have used AUC of 15

Key concept: The Calvert formula (Dose = AUC × [GFR + 25]) personalises carboplatin dosing based on renal function. The constant 25 accounts for non-renal clearance.
summary: Dose = BSA × Dose per m² = 1.9 m² × 400 mg/m² = 760 mg.
key_points: Body surface area (BSA): 1.9 m² | Dose per m²: 400 mg/m² | Total dose = BSA × mg/m² | = 1.9 × 400 = 760 mg
clinical_pearl: Carboplatin is often dosed by AUC (Calvert formula) rather than BSA: Dose = AUC × (GFR + 25). BSA dosing is less common for carboplatin but used for many other chemotherapy agents. Always verify dosing method.
exam_tip: BSA dosing: Dose = BSA × mg/m². Carboplatin often uses Calvert formula (AUC-based) instead - check which method is specified.
related_topics: BSA dosing | Chemotherapy | Carboplatin | Calvert formula

question_text: A 24 kg child is experiencing anaphylaxis. The recommended IM adrenaline dose is 10 micrograms/kg (maximum 500 micrograms). Adrenaline is available as 1:1000 solution. Calculate the volume to administer.
explanation:
**Ratio Calculation**

Compare the values and express as a ratio.

**Answer: 500 mg**
summary: 487 mg rounded to nearest 50 mg = 500 mg. 487 is closer to 500 than to 450.
key_points: Calculated dose: 487 mg | Options: round to 450 or 500 mg | 487 - 450 = 37 mg away from 450 | 500 - 487 = 13 mg away from 500 → round to 500
why_wrong_a: 165 mg is far below the calculated dose - significant underdose
why_wrong_b: 250 mg is approximately half the required dose
why_wrong_d: 750 mg is 54% higher than calculated - significant overdose
why_wrong_e: 5000 mg is 10× the required dose - dangerous
clinical_pearl: Practical rounding is necessary when calculated doses don't match available tablet/vial sizes. Generally acceptable to round within 10% of calculated dose. Document rounding rationale.
exam_tip: Round to nearest: find which multiple is closest. If exactly halfway, round up by convention.
related_topics: Dose rounding | Practical prescribing | Tablet strengths

question_text: A patient is started on lamotrigine for epilepsy (monotherapy titration) using the following titration schedule: 25 mg daily for 14 days; 50 mg daily for 14 days; 100 mg daily for 14 days; 200 mg daily for 28 days. Lamotrigine is available as 25 mg tablets. Calculate the total milligrams of lamotrigine over the entire course.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total mg for each phase
• Phase 1: 25 mg/day × 14 days = 350 mg
• Phase 2: 50 mg/day × 14 days = 700 mg
• Phase 3: 100 mg/day × 14 days = 1400 mg
• Phase 4: 200 mg/day × 28 days = 5600 mg

Step 2: Calculate the total
• Total = 350 + 700 + 1400 + 5600 = 8050 mg

Answer: 8050 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 805 mg — Decimal error (÷ 10)
• 2680.7 mg — Calculation error
• 2012.5 mg — May have used different durations
• 5369.4 mg — Calculation error
summary: Days = Total tablets / tablets per day = 90 / 2 = 45 days.
key_points: Tablets dispensed: 90 | Dose: 2 tablets daily | Supply: 90 ÷ 2 = 45 days
clinical_pearl: Days supply calculations important for prescription validity, refill timing, and adherence monitoring.
exam_tip: Days = Quantity ÷ Daily dose. Simple division.
related_topics: Days supply | Prescription calculations | Dispensing

question_text: A patient is started on levetiracetam for epilepsy (initiation) using the following titration schedule: 250 mg twice daily for 14 days; 500 mg twice daily for 14 days; 750 mg twice daily for 14 days; 1000 mg twice daily for 28 days. Levetiracetam is available as 250 mg tablets. Tablets come in packs of 60. How many packs should be dispensed for the primary drug?
explanation: Tablets/doses per day = 1. Duration = 1 days. Total = 1 × 1 = 0.
summary: Dose needed: 12.5 mg. Tablet: 25 mg (scored). Tablets = 12.5/25 = 0.5 tablet.
key_points: Required dose: 12.5 mg | Tablet strength: 25 mg | Calculation: 12.5 ÷ 25 = 0.5 tablet
clinical_pearl: Only split scored tablets. Unscored tablets may not divide evenly. Some formulations (extended-release) should never be split.
exam_tip: Tablets needed = dose required ÷ tablet strength. Scored tablets can be halved.
related_topics: Tablet calculations | Scored tablets | Dose fractions

question_text: A patient is started on rifampicin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide + ethambutol for TB (intensive phase, 50-70 kg patient) using the following titration schedule: R 450mg + H 300mg + Z 1500mg + E 800mg daily for 56 days. Rifampicin is available as 150 mg tablets.  isoniazid 100mg tabs (packs of 56); pyrazinamide 500mg tabs (packs of 100); ethambutol 400mg tabs (packs of 56); Tablets come in packs of 100. How many packs should be dispensed for the primary drug?
explanation: Tablets/doses per day = 1. Duration = 28 days. Total = 1 × 28 = 84.
summary: Total = Daily dose × Days = 3 tablets × 28 days = 84 tablets.
key_points: Daily dose: 3 tablets | Duration: 28 days | Total: 3 × 28 = 84 tablets
clinical_pearl: 28-day supplies are standard for monthly prescriptions. Check for maximum quantity limits on controlled drugs.
exam_tip: Quantity = Daily dose × Days. Simple multiplication.
related_topics: Quantity to dispense | Prescription calculations | Supply duration

question_text: A patient has a haemoglobin of 75 g/L. The target is 90 g/L. Each unit of packed red cells is expected to raise Hb by approximately 10 g/L. Each unit has a volume of approximately 300 mL and should be transfused over 3 hours. How many units are needed?
explanation: Adjusted Body Weight (ABW) accounts for drug distribution in obese patients. Lipophilic drugs distribute into adipose tissue, but not fully. Formula: ABW = IBW + 0.4 × (Actual - IBW). Calculation: ABW = 70 + 0.4 × (120 - 70) = 70 + 20 = 90 kg. The 0.4 factor assumes 40% distribution into excess adipose tissue.
key_points: Actual weight: 120 kg | Ideal body weight: 70 kg | ABW = 70 + 0.4 × 50 = 70 + 20 = 90 kg
clinical_pearl: ABW is used for drugs that distribute into lean and some adipose tissue (e.g., aminoglycosides). Factor 0.4 reflects ~40% distribution into fat.
exam_tip: ABW formula: IBW + 0.4 × (Actual - IBW). Used when actual weight significantly exceeds ideal.
related_topics: Adjusted body weight | Obesity dosing | Aminoglycosides | Pharmacokinetics

question_text: A patient is started on doxycycline + metronidazole for pelvic inflammatory disease using the following titration schedule: doxycycline 100 mg BD + metronidazole 400 mg BD for 14 days. Doxycycline is available as 100 mg tablets.  Also dispensing metronidazole 400 mg (packs of 21). How many packs of metronidazole 400 mg are needed?
explanation: Per dose = 500 ÷ 250 = 2.0 tablets. Total = 2.0 × 3 × 7 = 42.0 tablets.
summary: 500mg dose ÷ 250mg tablets = 2 tablets per dose. 2 × 3 × 7 = 42 tablets.
key_points: Dose per administration: 500 mg | Tablet strength: 250 mg | Tablets per dose = 500 ÷ 250 = 2 tablets | Total = 2 × 3 (TDS) × 7 days = 42 tablets
why_wrong_d: 52 tablets - not achievable with standard regimen
why_wrong_e: 126 tablets = 6 tablets per dose
clinical_pearl: When prescribed dose exceeds tablet strength, calculate tablets per dose first. This is common with antibiotics where higher doses are often needed but smaller tablet strengths are available.
exam_tip: Two-step: (1) Tablets per dose = Dose ÷ Tablet strength, (2) Total = Tablets per dose × frequency × days.
related_topics: Tablet calculations | Dose strength | Supply calculations | Antibiotic courses

question_text: A 105 kg patient requires 10 mL of 0.5% lidocaine (without adrenaline) for local anaesthesia. The stock solution is lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL). The BNF maximum dose of lidocaine without adrenaline is 3 mg/kg. How many mL of stock solution are needed to prepare this?
explanation: Weekly warfarin total: Add each daily dose: 3 + 3 + 2 + 3 + 2 + 3 + 2 = 18 mg per week. Warfarin dosing often varies day-to-day to achieve precise weekly totals. This equals an average of 18 ÷ 7 = 2.57 mg/day. Alternating doses (e.g., 3mg/2mg) allows finer INR control than fixed daily doses.
key_points: Mon: 3, Tue: 3, Wed: 2, Thu: 3, Fri: 2, Sat: 3, Sun: 2 | Total: 3+3+2+3+2+3+2 = 18 mg/week
clinical_pearl: Variable daily dosing achieves precise weekly totals. Average daily dose = 18/7 = 2.57 mg. Helps fine-tune INR control.
exam_tip: Add all daily doses for weekly total. Check your addition twice.
related_topics: Warfarin dosing | Weekly dose | INR management

question_text: A 22 kg child is experiencing anaphylaxis. The recommended IM adrenaline dose is 10 micrograms/kg (maximum 500 micrograms). Adrenaline is available as 1:1000 solution. Calculate the volume to administer.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the dose
• Dose = Weight × Dose per kg
• 22 kg × 10 mcg/kg = 220 micrograms

Step 2: Check against the maximum dose
• 220 mcg is below the maximum of 500 mcg, so no cap is needed

Step 3: Determine the concentration of 1:1000 adrenaline
• 1:1000 = 1 mg per 1 mL = 1000 micrograms per mL

Step 4: Calculate the volume
• Volume = Dose ÷ Concentration = 220 mcg ÷ 1000 mcg/mL = 0.22 mL

Answer: 0.22 mL of 1:1000 adrenaline IM

Why other answers are wrong:
• 0.44 mL — Double the correct answer
• 0.66 mL — Triple the correct answer
• 0.2 mL — Would deliver 200 mcg (approximately 9 mcg/kg)
• 0.02 mL — Decimal error (÷ 10)

Key concept: 1:1000 adrenaline = 1 mg/mL = 1000 mcg/mL. Used IM for anaphylaxis.
summary: Oral:IV ratio = 3:1. IV dose = Oral ÷ 3 = 60 mg ÷ 3 = 20 mg.
key_points: Oral morphine: 60 mg | Conversion ratio: 3:1 (oral:IV) | IV dose: 60 ÷ 3 = 20 mg
clinical_pearl: Morphine oral bioavailability ~30%. Always reduce dose when switching oral to IV. Titrate to effect.
exam_tip: Oral:IV = 3:1 means oral dose is 3× the IV. To convert oral→IV, divide by 3.
related_topics: Opioid conversions | Morphine equivalents | Route conversions

question_text: A 68 kg patient with an eGFR of 22 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed gentamicin at 5 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 500 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 15-29 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 40% of normal dose. Gentamicin injection is available as 40 mg/mL in 2 mL vials. What volume of gentamicin injection (40 mg/mL) should be drawn up?
explanation: Reverse dose calculation: To find dose per kg when total dose is known, use: Dose (mg/kg) = Total dose ÷ Weight. Calculation: 420 mg ÷ 60 kg = 7 mg/kg. This is useful for verifying prescribed doses against recommended ranges or when documenting doses for patients of different weights.
summary: Reverse calculation: 420 mg ÷ 60 kg = 7 mg/kg
key_points: mg/kg = Total dose ÷ Weight | 420 ÷ 60 = 7 mg/kg | Useful for verifying doses | Compare against recommended ranges
why_wrong_a: 2 mg/kg would only be 120 mg total
why_wrong_e: 70 mg/kg would be 4200 mg total
clinical_pearl: Reverse calculating mg/kg helps verify if a prescribed dose falls within safe ranges. Always check against BNF recommended doses, especially for high-risk medications.
exam_tip: To verify a dose: divide total mg by kg. Compare result to recommended mg/kg range in BNF.
related_topics: Dose verification | Medication safety | Weight-based dosing | BNF reference

question_text: A 72 kg patient with an eGFR of 32 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed vancomycin at 15 mg/kg every 12 hours. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 2000 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 30-60 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 75% of normal dose. Vancomycin injection is available as 50 mg/mL in 10 mL vials. What is the correct vancomycin dose for this patient?
explanation: Loading dose = weight × dose per kg = 80 kg × 5 mg/kg = 400 mg.
summary: Loading dose = 5 mg/kg × 80 kg = 400 mg.
key_points: Dose: 5 mg/kg | Weight: 80 kg | Loading dose: 5 × 80 = 400 mg
clinical_pearl: Aminophylline = theophylline + ethylenediamine. Loading dose achieves therapeutic levels quickly. Reduce if patient already on theophylline.
exam_tip: Simple weight-based calculation. Remember to check if patient is theophylline-naive.
related_topics: Aminophylline | Loading doses | Theophylline | COPD/asthma

question_text: A patient is started on pregabalin for generalised anxiety disorder using the following titration schedule: 75 mg twice daily for 7 days; 150 mg twice daily for 7 days; 300 mg twice daily for 14 days. Pregabalin is available as 75 mg tablets. Tablets come in packs of 56. How many packs should be dispensed for the primary drug?
explanation:
**Total Dose Calculation**

**Formula:** Total = Single dose × Frequency × Duration

**Answer: 10 g**
key_points: Dose: 500 mg three times daily | Duration: 7 days | Total mg = 500 × 3 × 7 = 10,500 mg | Convert to g: 10,500 ÷ 1000 = 10.5g ≈ 10g
why_wrong_e: 5 g = 500mg TDS × 3.3 days
clinical_pearl: Calculating total drug quantity helps with procurement and cost calculations. Convert mg to g by dividing by 1000. Useful for checking total antibiotic exposure or drug budgeting.
exam_tip: Total = Dose × Frequency × Days. Then convert: mg ÷ 1000 = g.
related_topics: Total dose calculations | Unit conversions | Drug quantities | Course calculations

question_text: A patient is started on duloxetine for diabetic neuropathy using the following titration schedule: 30 mg daily for 14 days; 60 mg daily for 14 days; 60 mg twice daily for 28 days. Duloxetine is available as 30 mg tablets. Calculate the total number of 30 mg tablets needed for the primary drug.
explanation: Units = volume × concentration = 0.4 mL × 100 units/mL = 40.0 units.
summary: U-100 = 100 units/mL. Units = 0.4 mL × 100 = 40 units.
key_points: U-100 means 100 units per mL | Volume: 0.4 mL | Calculation: 0.4 × 100 = 40 units | Use insulin syringes for accurate measurement
why_wrong_e: 20 units would need 0.2 mL
clinical_pearl: U-100 is standard insulin concentration (100 units/mL). U-500 (5× concentrated) exists for insulin-resistant patients - HIGH RISK for errors. Always use insulin syringes matched to concentration. Never use standard syringes for insulin.
exam_tip: U-100 = 100 units/mL. Units = Volume(mL) × 100. Insulin is HIGH-RISK - always double-check calculations.
related_topics: Insulin dosing | High-risk medications | U-100 vs U-500 | Diabetes management

question_text: A 2-year-old child weighing 8 kg is prescribed cefalexin at 25 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses (BD) for 14 days. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of N/A mg and maximum daily dose of 2000 mg for children. Cefalexin is available as 125 mg/5 mL suspension. The suspension comes in 100 mL bottles. How many 100 mL bottles of 125 mg/5 mL suspension should be dispensed?
explanation: The paracetamol level of 180 mg/L is above the treatment threshold of 150 mg/L at 4 hours. N-acetylcysteine treatment is indicated.
summary: 180 mg/L is above the treatment line (150 mg/L at 4 hours). N-acetylcysteine treatment is indicated.
key_points: Patient level: 180 mg/L at 4 hours | Treatment line at 4 hours: 150 mg/L | 180 > 150 = above treatment line | Treatment with N-acetylcysteine is required
why_wrong_b: Level is ABOVE, not below the treatment line
why_wrong_c: Treatment should start immediately, not wait for repeat
why_wrong_d: Treatment is based on level, not symptoms
why_wrong_e: Waiting for 8-hour level wastes critical time
clinical_pearl: The paracetamol treatment nomogram starts at 4 hours post-ingestion. UK uses a single treatment line (100 mg/L at 4h, or 150 mg/L historically). NAC is most effective within 8 hours but can be given up to 24 hours post-ingestion.
exam_tip: Compare level to treatment line at the SAME time point. If above = treat. If below = generally no treatment needed (with caveats for staggered overdose).
related_topics: Paracetamol overdose | Treatment nomogram | N-acetylcysteine | Toxicology

question_text: A patient (weight 60 kg, height 162 cm, BSA 1.55 m²) is prescribed methotrexate (high-dose) at 3000 mg/m² as part of a chemotherapy regimen. The BNF maximum single dose is 8000 mg. The calculated dose is to be diluted in 500 mL of appropriate diluent and infused over 24 hour(s). Calculate the correct dose of methotrexate (high-dose) for this patient.
explanation: Body Surface Area (BSA) using Mosteller formula: BSA (m²) = √(Weight(kg) × Height(cm) ÷ 3600). Calculation: BSA = √(70 × 170 ÷ 3600) = √(11900 ÷ 3600) = √3.306 = 1.82 m². BSA is used for dosing chemotherapy and other drugs where body size significantly affects pharmacokinetics. Average adult BSA is approximately 1.7-1.9 m².
summary: BSA using Mosteller: √(70 × 170 ÷ 3600) = √3.306 = 1.82 m².
key_points: Mosteller formula: BSA = √(weight × height / 3600) | Weight in kg, height in cm | 70 × 170 = 11,900 | 11,900 ÷ 3600 = 3.306; √3.306 = 1.82 m²
why_wrong_a: 2.27 m² is too high - would require larger weight/height
why_wrong_c: 2.73 m² is far too high - nearly double the correct answer
why_wrong_d: 1.36 m² is too low - typically paediatric range
why_wrong_e: 0.91 m² is far too low - infant range
clinical_pearl: Average adult BSA is approximately 1.7-1.9 m². BSA is essential for chemotherapy dosing and some renal function calculations. The Mosteller formula is simpler than Du Bois.
exam_tip: Mosteller: √(kg × cm ÷ 3600). Average adult ≈ 1.7-1.9 m². If your answer is <1 or >2.5, recheck.
related_topics: BSA calculation | Mosteller formula | Chemotherapy dosing

question_text: A patient is started on ramipril for heart failure (up-titration) using the following titration schedule: 1.25 mg daily for 7 days; 2.5 mg daily for 7 days; 5 mg daily for 7 days; 10 mg daily for 28 days. Ramipril is available as 1.25 mg tablets. Calculate the total number of 1.25 mg tablets needed for the primary drug.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total number of 1.25 mg tablets for each phase
• Phase 1: 1.25 mg daily × 7 days → 1 tablet/day × 7 = 7 tablets
• Phase 2: 2.5 mg daily × 7 days → 2 tablets/day × 7 = 14 tablets
• Phase 3: 5 mg daily × 7 days → 4 tablets/day × 7 = 28 tablets
• Phase 4: 10 mg daily × 28 days → 8 tablets/day × 28 = 224 tablets

Step 2: Calculate the total tablets
• Total = 7 + 14 + 28 + 224 = 273 tablets

Answer: 273 tablets

Why other answers are wrong:
• 27.3 tablets — Decimal error (÷ 10)
• 136.5 tablets — Half the correct answer
• 546 tablets — Double the correct answer
• 90.91 tablets — Calculation error
summary: Prednisolone taper: (40 - 10) ÷ 5 mg/week = 6 weeks
key_points: Reduction needed = 40 - 10 = 30 mg | 30 ÷ 5 = 6 weeks | Gradual taper prevents adrenal crisis | HPA axis needs time to recover
why_wrong_e: 3 weeks would require 10 mg/week reduction
clinical_pearl: Steroid tapering is essential after >3 weeks of treatment to allow HPA axis recovery. Too rapid reduction risks adrenal insufficiency. Some patients need slower tapers with smaller decrements at lower doses.
exam_tip: Taper duration = Total reduction ÷ Weekly reduction. Slower tapers may be needed at lower doses.
related_topics: Corticosteroid tapering | Adrenal suppression | HPA axis | Steroid withdrawal

question_text: A 65 kg patient with an eGFR of 15 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed amikacin at 15 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 1500 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 15-29 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 40% of normal dose. Amikacin injection is available as 50 mg/mL in 2 mL vials. The dose is diluted to 250 mL and infused over 90 minutes. If the adjusted dose is diluted to 250 mL and infused over 90 minutes, what is the infusion rate in mL/hour?
summary: Daily: 25 mg/kg × 20 kg = 500 mg. Per dose: 500 ÷ 3 = 166.7 mg. Volume: 166.7 ÷ 25 = 6.67 ≈ 6.7 mL.
key_points: Daily: 25 × 20 = 500 mg | Per dose (÷3): 166.7 mg | Suspension: 125 mg/5 mL = 25 mg/mL | Volume: 166.7 ÷ 25 = 6.67 mL
clinical_pearl: Pediatric suspensions require careful calculation and measurement. Use oral syringes for accuracy, not household spoons.
exam_tip: Three steps: (1) daily dose, (2) per-dose amount, (3) convert to volume using concentration.
related_topics: Pediatric dosing | Oral suspensions | Volume calculations

question_text: A 82 kg patient with an eGFR of 10 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed amikacin at 15 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 1500 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 0-14 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 25% of normal dose. Amikacin injection is available as 50 mg/mL in 2 mL vials. The dose is diluted to 100 mL and infused over 90 minutes. If the adjusted dose is diluted to 100 mL and infused over 90 minutes, what is the infusion rate in mL/hour?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the normal dose
• Normal dose = 82 kg × 15 mg/kg = 1230 mg

Step 2: Check against the maximum single dose
• 1230 mg is below 1500 mg maximum, so no cap

Step 3: Apply renal dose adjustment
• eGFR 10 falls in 0–14 range: reduce to 25%
• Adjusted dose = 1230 mg × 0.25 = 307.5 mg

Step 4: Calculate the infusion rate
• Diluted to 100 mL, infused over 90 minutes (1.5 hours)
• Rate = 100 mL ÷ 1.5 hours = 66.7 mL/hour

Answer: 66.7 mL/hour

Why other answers are wrong:
• 6.67 mL/hour — Decimal error (÷ 10)
• 266.8 mL/hour — Calculation error
• 60.03 mL/hour — Calculation error
• 200.1 mL/hour — Would imply a 30-minute infusion
summary: Loading dose = 18 mg/kg × 70 kg = 1260 mg.
key_points: Dose: 18 mg/kg | Weight: 70 kg | Loading: 18 × 70 = 1260 mg
clinical_pearl: Phenytoin loading 15-20 mg/kg. Give IV at max 50 mg/min to avoid hypotension/arrhythmias. Oral loading possible in divided doses.
exam_tip: Simple multiplication. Phenytoin loading is weight-based with defined range.
related_topics: Phenytoin | Loading doses | Status epilepticus

question_text: A 70 kg patient requires 10 mL of 0.25% lidocaine (without adrenaline) for local anaesthesia. The stock solution is lidocaine 2% (20 mg/mL). The BNF maximum dose of lidocaine without adrenaline is 3 mg/kg. What is the maximum safe dose for this patient in mg?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the maximum safe dose
• Maximum dose = Weight × Maximum dose per kg
• 70 kg × 3 mg/kg = 210 mg

Step 2: For reference, check the dose in the prepared solution
• 0.25% lidocaine = 2.5 mg/mL
• Dose in 10 mL = 10 × 2.5 = 25 mg (well within 210 mg max)

Answer: The maximum safe dose is 210 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 840 mg — May have used 12 mg/kg
• 52.5 mg — May have used 0.75 mg/kg
• 315 mg — May have used 4.5 mg/kg
• 420 mg — May have used 6 mg/kg
summary: Codeine:Morphine = 10:1. Morphine equivalent = 240 mg ÷ 10 = 24 mg.
key_points: Codeine dose: 240 mg daily | Conversion ratio: 10:1 (codeine:morphine) | Morphine equivalent: 240 ÷ 10 = 24 mg
clinical_pearl: Codeine is a prodrug converted to morphine by CYP2D6. Poor metabolizers get little analgesia; ultra-rapid metabolizers risk toxicity.
exam_tip: Codeine to morphine: divide by 10. Morphine to codeine: multiply by 10.
related_topics: Opioid conversions | Codeine | CYP2D6 metabolism

question_text: A patient is started on prednisolone for acute asthma exacerbation using the following titration schedule: 40 mg daily for 5 days; 30 mg daily for 3 days; 20 mg daily for 3 days; 10 mg daily for 3 days; 5 mg daily for 3 days. Prednisolone is available as 5 mg tablets. Calculate the total milligrams of prednisolone over the entire course.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total mg for each phase
• Phase 1: 40 mg/day × 5 days = 200 mg
• Phase 2: 30 mg/day × 3 days = 90 mg
• Phase 3: 20 mg/day × 3 days = 60 mg
• Phase 4: 10 mg/day × 3 days = 30 mg
• Phase 5: 5 mg/day × 3 days = 15 mg

Step 2: Calculate the total
• Total = 200 + 90 + 60 + 30 + 15 = 395 mg

Answer: 395 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 98.75 mg — Quarter of the correct answer
• 1580 mg — Quadruple the correct answer
• 197.5 mg — Half the correct answer
• 790 mg — Double the correct answer
summary: Daily use: 2 tablets × 2 = 4 tablets. Days: 56 ÷ 4 = 14 days.
key_points: Dose: 2 tablets twice daily = 4/day | Quantity: 56 tablets | Supply: 56 ÷ 4 = 14 days
clinical_pearl: Days supply calculations important for prescription validity, refill timing, and insurance coverage.
exam_tip: Days = Total quantity ÷ Daily consumption.
related_topics: Days supply | Prescription calculations | Dispensing

question_text: A patient is started on venlafaxine MR for depression (switching) using the following titration schedule: 75 mg daily for 14 days; 150 mg daily for 14 days; 225 mg daily for 28 days. Venlafaxine is available as 75 mg tablets. Tablets come in packs of 30. How many packs should be dispensed for the primary drug?
explanation: Tablets per dose = 250 ÷ 250 = 1. Total = 1 × 4 × 5 = 20 tablets.
summary: Daily: 250 mg × 4 = 1000 mg (4 tablets). Course: 4 × 5 days = 20 tablets.
key_points: Dose: 250 mg QDS (4 times daily) | Tablet: 250 mg | Daily tablets: 4 | 5-day course: 4 × 5 = 20 tablets
clinical_pearl: Always dispense exact quantity for antibiotic courses. Incomplete courses risk resistance; excess risks inappropriate future self-medication.
exam_tip: Total = tablets per day × days. Here: 4/day × 5 days = 20.
related_topics: Course calculations | Quantity to dispense | Antibiotic courses

question_text: A 95 kg patient with an eGFR of 40 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed amikacin at 15 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 1500 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 30-60 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 60% of normal dose. Amikacin injection is available as 50 mg/mL in 2 mL vials. What volume of amikacin injection (50 mg/mL) should be drawn up?
explanation: Immunosuppressant weight-based dosing: Single dose = Weight × Dose per kg = 65 kg × 3 mg/kg = 195 mg per dose. Daily total = 195 mg × 2 = 390 mg/day. Ciclosporin requires therapeutic drug monitoring due to narrow therapeutic index and significant inter-patient variability. Trough levels guide dose adjustments.
summary: Single dose = 3 mg/kg × 65 kg = 195 mg (given twice daily).
key_points: Dose: 3 mg/kg per dose | Weight: 65 kg | Single dose: 3 × 65 = 195 mg | Given twice daily
why_wrong_a: 390 mg would be total daily dose
clinical_pearl: Ciclosporin requires TDM (trough 100-200 ng/mL for transplant). Highly variable absorption. Many drug interactions via CYP3A4.
exam_tip: Question asks for single dose, not daily total. Read carefully.
related_topics: Ciclosporin | Immunosuppressants | TDM | Transplant medicine

question_text: A 65 kg patient with an eGFR of 28 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed gentamicin at 5 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 500 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 15-29 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 40% of normal dose. Gentamicin injection is available as 40 mg/mL in 2 mL vials. The dose is diluted to 250 mL and infused over 60 minutes. If the adjusted dose is diluted to 250 mL and infused over 60 minutes, what is the infusion rate in mL/hour?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the normal dose
• Normal dose = 65 kg × 5 mg/kg = 325 mg

Step 2: Check against the maximum single dose
• 325 mg is below 500 mg maximum, so no cap

Step 3: Apply renal dose adjustment
• eGFR 28 falls in 15–29 range: reduce to 40%
• Adjusted dose = 325 mg × 0.40 = 130 mg

Step 4: Calculate the infusion rate
• Diluted to 250 mL, infused over 60 minutes (1 hour)
• Rate = 250 mL ÷ 1 hour = 250 mL/hour

Answer: 250 mL/hour

Why other answers are wrong:
• 62.5 mL/hour — Would imply a 4-hour infusion
• 225 mL/hour — Calculation error
• 83.25 mL/hour — Would imply a 3-hour infusion
• 166.8 mL/hour — Would imply a 90-minute infusion
summary: 70 kg × 7 mg/kg = 490 mg once daily (extended-interval dosing).
key_points: Patient weight: 70 kg | Dose: 7 mg/kg once daily | Calculation: 70 × 7 = 490 mg | Extended-interval = single daily dose
clinical_pearl: Extended-interval (once-daily) aminoglycoside dosing exploits concentration-dependent killing and post-antibiotic effect. Higher peaks improve efficacy; the drug-free interval reduces nephrotoxicity.
exam_tip: Weight-based dosing: Weight (kg) × Dose (mg/kg) = Total dose (mg). Extended-interval gentamicin is typically 5-7 mg/kg once daily.
related_topics: Aminoglycoside dosing | Weight-based calculations | Extended-interval dosing | Therapeutic drug monitoring

question_text: A 85 kg patient with an eGFR of 38 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed amikacin at 15 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 1500 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 30-60 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 60% of normal dose. Amikacin injection is available as 50 mg/mL in 2 mL vials. The dose is diluted to 500 mL and infused over 60 minutes. If the adjusted dose is diluted to 500 mL and infused over 60 minutes, what is the infusion rate in mL/hour?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the normal dose
• Normal dose = 85 kg × 15 mg/kg = 1275 mg

Step 2: Check against the maximum single dose
• 1275 mg is below 1500 mg maximum, so no cap

Step 3: Apply renal dose adjustment
• eGFR 38 falls in 30–60 range: reduce to 60%
• Adjusted dose = 1275 mg × 0.60 = 765 mg

Step 4: Calculate the infusion rate
• Diluted to 500 mL, infused over 60 minutes (1 hour)
• Rate = 500 mL ÷ 1 hour = 500 mL/hour

Answer: 500 mL/hour

Why other answers are wrong:
• 250 mL/hour — Would imply a 2-hour infusion
• 50 mL/hour — Would imply a 10-hour infusion
• 125 mL/hour — Would imply a 4-hour infusion
• 166.5 mL/hour — Would imply a 3-hour infusion
summary: Treatment dose = 1.5 mg/kg × 80 kg = 120 mg once daily.
key_points: Dose: 1.5 mg/kg once daily | Weight: 80 kg | Dose: 1.5 × 80 = 120 mg
clinical_pearl: Treatment-dose enoxaparin: 1.5 mg/kg OD or 1 mg/kg BD. Prophylactic dose is typically 40 mg OD. No monitoring usually needed.
exam_tip: Simple multiplication. Know treatment vs prophylactic doses for LMWH.
related_topics: Enoxaparin | LMWH dosing | VTE treatment | Anticoagulation

question_text: A 65 kg patient in the ICU requires dobutamine at 2.5 micrograms/kg/minute. The infusion is prepared as 250 mg in 250 mL (concentration: 1 mg/mL). The maximum recommended rate is 20 micrograms/kg/minute. Calculate the infusion rate in mL/hour.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the dose per minute
• Dose = Weight × Dose rate = 65 kg × 2.5 mcg/kg/min = 162.5 mcg/min

Step 2: Convert to mg per hour
• 162.5 mcg/min × 60 = 9750 mcg/hour = 9.75 mg/hour

Step 3: Calculate the infusion rate
• Concentration = 1 mg/mL (250 mg in 250 mL)
• Rate = 9.75 mg/hr ÷ 1 mg/mL = 9.75 mL/hour

Step 4: Confirm within maximum
• Maximum at 20 mcg/kg/min = 65 × 20 × 60 ÷ 1000 = 78 mL/hr
• 9.75 mL/hour is within maximum

Answer: 9.75 mL/hour

Why other answers are wrong:
• 14.62 mL/hour — May have used 3.75 mcg/kg/min
• 3.25 mL/hour — May have omitted the ×60 conversion
• 2.44 mL/hour — Calculation error
• 39 mL/hour — Would require 10 mcg/kg/min
summary: Current: 500 mg × 3 = 1500 mg/day. Max: 2550 mg. Headroom: 2550 - 1500 = 1050 mg/day.
key_points: Current: 500 mg TDS = 1500 mg/day | Maximum: 2550 mg/day | Available increase: 2550 - 1500 = 1050 mg
why_wrong_b: 2550 mg is total max, not headroom
clinical_pearl: Metformin max is 2550 mg/day (some guidelines 3000 mg). Higher doses have limited additional efficacy but more GI side effects.
exam_tip: Calculate current total, subtract from maximum to find available headroom.
related_topics: Metformin | Maximum doses | Diabetes management

question_text: A patient is started on bisoprolol for heart failure (up-titration) using the following titration schedule: 1.25 mg daily for 14 days; 2.5 mg daily for 14 days; 3.75 mg daily for 14 days; 5 mg daily for 14 days; 7.5 mg daily for 14 days; 10 mg daily for 28 days. Bisoprolol is available as 1.25 mg tablets. Calculate the total milligrams of bisoprolol over the entire course.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total mg for each phase
• Phase 1: 1.25 mg/day × 14 days = 17.5 mg
• Phase 2: 2.5 mg/day × 14 days = 35 mg
• Phase 3: 3.75 mg/day × 14 days = 52.5 mg
• Phase 4: 5 mg/day × 14 days = 70 mg
• Phase 5: 7.5 mg/day × 14 days = 105 mg
• Phase 6: 10 mg/day × 28 days = 280 mg

Step 2: Calculate the total
• Total = 17.5 + 35 + 52.5 + 70 + 105 + 280 = 560 mg

Answer: 560 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 504 mg — Calculation error
• 373.5 mg — May have used shorter durations
• 186.5 mg — Approximately one-third of the total
• 840 mg — 1.5× the correct answer
summary: Patient 45 kg < 50 kg threshold. Use 450 mg dose.
key_points: Weight: 45 kg | Threshold: 50 kg | 45 < 50, so use lower band | Dose: 450 mg
why_wrong_c: 500 mg is not a standard band
clinical_pearl: Rifampicin uses weight bands, not continuous weight-based dosing. This simplifies prescribing for TB treatment.
exam_tip: Weight-band dosing: check which band patient falls into. Here <50 kg = 450 mg.
related_topics: TB treatment | Weight-band dosing | Rifampicin

question_text: A 62 kg patient with an eGFR of 48 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed fluconazole at 6 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 400 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 30-50 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 50% of normal dose. Fluconazole injection is available as 2 mg/mL in 100 mL vials. The dose is diluted to 250 mL and infused over 90 minutes. If the adjusted dose is diluted to 250 mL and infused over 90 minutes, what is the infusion rate in mL/hour?
explanation: Dose = weight × dose per kg = 75 × 0.1 = 7.5 mg.
summary: Midazolam 0.1 mg/kg for 75 kg patient = 7.5 mg. Simple multiplication: 75 × 0.1 = 7.5 mg.
key_points: Weight-based dosing for sedation | Dose = Weight × Dose per kg | 75 kg × 0.1 mg/kg = 7.5 mg | Standard sedation dose range
why_wrong_b: 9.8 mg has no mathematical relationship to the given values
why_wrong_c: 5.2 mg has no mathematical relationship to the given values
why_wrong_d: 15 mg would be 0.2 mg/kg - double the stated dose
why_wrong_e: 3.8 mg has no mathematical relationship to the given values
clinical_pearl: Midazolam has rapid onset but short duration. In elderly or hepatically impaired patients, reduce dose by 30-50%. Monitor for respiratory depression, especially with opioids.
exam_tip: mg/kg × kg = mg. Keep it simple - multiply weight by dose rate.
related_topics: Sedation dosing | Benzodiazepines | Weight-based calculations

question_text: A 105 kg patient with an eGFR of 18 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed vancomycin at 15 mg/kg every 12 hours. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 2000 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 15-29 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 50% of normal dose. Vancomycin injection is available as 50 mg/mL in 10 mL vials. The dose is diluted to 250 mL and infused over 120 minutes. If the adjusted dose is diluted to 250 mL and infused over 120 minutes, what is the infusion rate in mL/hour?
explanation: For INR 4.5-5.0 without bleeding, omit 1-2 doses and reduce the maintenance dose. Vitamin K is reserved for higher INRs (>8) or bleeding.
summary: INR 4.8 (target 2-3) without bleeding: omit 1-2 doses, then reduce maintenance dose.
key_points: INR: 4.8 (supratherapeutic) | Target range: 2-3 | No bleeding = no urgent reversal needed | Action: omit 1-2 doses, reduce maintenance
why_wrong_b: Same dose would maintain elevated INR - risk of bleeding
why_wrong_d: Permanent stop not indicated for reversible INR elevation
why_wrong_e: IV vitamin K 10mg reserved for major bleeding or very high INR
clinical_pearl: Warfarin management by INR: 4.5-6 = omit 1-2 doses; 6-8 = omit doses ± low-dose vitamin K (1-2.5mg); >8 = vitamin K 1-5mg oral; major bleeding = vitamin K 5mg IV + PCC. Always check for bleeding.
exam_tip: INR management: assess for bleeding first. Vitamin K for INR>8 or bleeding. Lower INR: omit and reduce.
related_topics: Warfarin management | INR monitoring | Anticoagulation reversal | Vitamin K

question_text: A 105 kg patient requires a drug with the following parameters: Vd = 0.5 L/kg, target Css = 20 mg/L, half-life = 12 hours, oral bioavailability = 80%. Calculate the oral loading dose.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the volume of distribution
• Vd = 0.5 L/kg × 105 kg = 52.5 L

Step 2: Calculate the IV loading dose
• IV Loading dose = Vd × Target Css
• IV LD = 52.5 L × 20 mg/L = 1050 mg

Step 3: Adjust for oral bioavailability
• Oral bioavailability = 80% = 0.8
• Oral loading dose = IV LD ÷ Bioavailability
• Oral LD = 1050 mg ÷ 0.8 = 1312.5 mg

Answer: 1312.5 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 656.2 mg — Half the correct answer
• 5250 mg — May have used Vd × Css × 4
• 875.4 mg — May have used a different bioavailability
• 328.1 mg — Quarter of the correct answer

Key concept: Oral LD = (Vd × Css) / F. The dose must be increased to compensate for incomplete absorption.
summary: Total volume = Doses × Volume per dose = 10 × 0.5 mL = 5 mL.
key_points: Doses: 10 | Volume per dose: 0.5 mL | Total: 10 × 0.5 = 5 mL
why_wrong_a: 10 mL would be 1 mL per dose
clinical_pearl: Multi-dose vials require careful tracking of doses used. Follow manufacturer guidance for storage after first puncture.
exam_tip: Simple multiplication. Total = number of doses × volume per dose.
related_topics: Vaccine vials | Multi-dose vials | Immunization

question_text: Gabapentin is started at 300 mg once daily, increasing by 300 mg every 3 days to 900 mg TDS. How many days to reach maintenance?
explanation: Starting at 300 mg OD → 300 BD → 300 TDS → 600 TDS → 900 TDS. Each step takes 3 days. Steps: 300→600→900→1200→1500→1800→2100→2700 (8 steps × 3 days = 24 days).
summary: Target: 900 mg TDS = 2700 mg/day. Start: 300 mg OD. Increase 300 mg every 3 days. Steps: 300→600→900→1200→1500→1800→2100→2400→2700. That's 8 increases × 3 days = 24 days.
key_points: Start: 300 mg daily | Target: 900 mg TDS (2700 mg/day) | Increase: 300 mg every 3 days | 8 steps × 3 days = 24 days
clinical_pearl: Gabapentin titration reduces side effects (dizziness, sedation). Faster titration possible if tolerated. Renal adjustment needed.
exam_tip: Count the number of 300 mg increments from start to target, multiply by interval.
related_topics: Gabapentin | Dose titration | Neuropathic pain

question_text: A patient is started on lamotrigine for epilepsy (monotherapy titration) using the following titration schedule: 25 mg daily for 14 days; 50 mg daily for 14 days; 100 mg daily for 14 days; 200 mg daily for 28 days. Lamotrigine is available as 25 mg tablets. Calculate the total number of 25 mg tablets needed for the primary drug.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total number of 25 mg tablets for each phase
• Phase 1: 25 mg daily × 14 days → 1 tablet/day × 14 = 14 tablets
• Phase 2: 50 mg daily × 14 days → 2 tablets/day × 14 = 28 tablets
• Phase 3: 100 mg daily × 14 days → 4 tablets/day × 14 = 56 tablets
• Phase 4: 200 mg daily × 28 days → 8 tablets/day × 28 = 224 tablets

Step 2: Calculate the total tablets
• Total = 14 + 28 + 56 + 224 = 322 tablets

Answer: 322 tablets

Why other answers are wrong:
• 354.2 tablets — Calculation error
• 214.8 tablets — May have used shorter durations
• 289.8 tablets — May have used 7 tablets/day in phase 4
• 1288 tablets — Quadruple the correct answer
summary: Trimethoprim supply: 2 tablets BD × 3 days = 6 tablets
key_points: BD = twice daily = 2 per day | 3 days × 2 = 6 tablets | Standard 3-day UTI course | 200 mg tablets
clinical_pearl: Trimethoprim 200 mg BD for 3 days is first-line for uncomplicated UTI in women. Avoid if CrCl <15 mL/min. Can cause hyperkalaemia - caution with ACE inhibitors/ARBs. Check local resistance patterns.
exam_tip: BD × 3 days = 6 doses. Trimethoprim 3-day course for uncomplicated UTI in women.
related_topics: UTI treatment | Antibiotic courses | Trimethoprim | Uncomplicated cystitis

question_text: A 62 kg patient with an eGFR of 15 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed fluconazole at 6 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 400 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 0-29 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 50% of normal dose. Fluconazole injection is available as 2 mg/mL in 100 mL vials. What is the correct fluconazole dose for this patient?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the normal dose
• Normal dose = 62 kg × 6 mg/kg = 372 mg

Step 2: Check against maximum
• 372 mg is below 400 mg max, so no cap

Step 3: Apply renal dose adjustment
• eGFR 15 falls in 0–29 range: reduce to 50%
• Adjusted dose = 372 mg × 0.50 = 186 mg

Answer: 186 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 558 mg — Triple the correct answer
• 204.6 mg — Calculation error
• 372 mg — No renal adjustment applied
• 46.5 mg — Quarter of the correct answer
summary: BG 16.5 mmol/L falls in 14-18 range. Dose: 6 units.
key_points: Blood glucose: 16.5 mmol/L | Range 14-18 mmol/L = 6 units | 16.5 is within 14-18 | Give 6 units
why_wrong_b: 8 units would be for higher BG
why_wrong_c: 2 units would be for lower BG
clinical_pearl: Sliding scales are reactive, not proactive. Modern practice favors basal-bolus regimens. Always check local protocol.
exam_tip: Find which range the value falls into. Apply the corresponding dose.
related_topics: Insulin sliding scale | Diabetes | Inpatient glucose management

question_text: A 65 kg palliative care patient is currently taking codeine 60 mg orally 6 times daily. The patient can no longer swallow and needs to be converted to SC morphine. The morphine will be given via a 24-hour syringe driver. Calculate the total 24-hour dose of SC morphine.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate total daily oral codeine
• Codeine 60 mg × 6 doses/day = 360 mg oral codeine per day

Step 2: Convert oral codeine to oral morphine equivalent
• Oral codeine to oral morphine ratio = 10:1
• Oral morphine equivalent = 360 mg ÷ 10 = 36 mg oral morphine per day

Step 3: Convert oral morphine to SC morphine
• Oral to SC morphine ratio = 2:1
• SC morphine = 36 mg ÷ 2 = 18 mg per 24 hours

Answer: 18 mg/24 hours via syringe driver

Why other answers are wrong:
• 27 mg/24 hours — May have used a 1.5:1 oral:SC ratio
• 180 mg/24 hours — May have only divided by 2 (not ÷10 then ÷2)
• 72 mg/24 hours — May have divided codeine by 5 instead of 10
• 1.8 mg/24 hours — Decimal error (÷ 10)

Key concept: Codeine ÷ 10 = oral morphine. Oral morphine ÷ 2 = SC morphine. So codeine ÷ 20 = SC morphine.
summary: Standard VTE prophylaxis dose of enoxaparin: 40 mg subcutaneously once daily.
key_points: Indication: VTE prophylaxis (medical patient) | Standard dose: 40 mg SC once daily | Not weight-based for prophylaxis
why_wrong_d: 80 mg would be treatment for 80 kg
clinical_pearl: Prophylactic enoxaparin: 40 mg OD (medical), 30 mg BD (post-surgery). Reduce to 20 mg in severe renal impairment.
exam_tip: Know fixed prophylactic doses vs weight-based treatment doses for LMWH.
related_topics: Enoxaparin | VTE prophylaxis | LMWH dosing

question_text: A 74 kg patient requires 10 mL of 0.5% lidocaine (without adrenaline) for local anaesthesia. The stock solution is lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL). The BNF maximum dose of lidocaine without adrenaline is 3 mg/kg. How many mL of stock solution are needed to prepare this?
explanation: Daily doses = 2 × 2 = 4. Days = 200 ÷ 4 = 50 days.
summary: Inhaler duration: 200 doses ÷ 4 puffs/day = 50 days
key_points: Daily puffs = 2 puffs × 2 times = 4 | Duration = 200 ÷ 4 = 50 days | Check dose counter regularly | Order refill before running out
why_wrong_e: 62 days would require ~3.2 puffs/day
clinical_pearl: Advise patients to check inhaler dose counters and order refills when ~20 doses remain. 50 days is less than 2 months - monthly prescriptions may not align with usage. Regular preventer use is key for asthma control.
exam_tip: Inhaler days = Total doses ÷ (puffs per dose × doses per day). Counsel on timely refills.
related_topics: Inhaler technique | Asthma management | Dose counters | Medication adherence

question_text: A 62 kg patient with an eGFR of 42 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed gentamicin at 5 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 500 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 30-60 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 60% of normal dose. Gentamicin injection is available as 40 mg/mL in 2 mL vials. The dose is diluted to 500 mL and infused over 60 minutes. If the adjusted dose is diluted to 500 mL and infused over 60 minutes, what is the infusion rate in mL/hour?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the normal dose
• Normal dose = 62 kg × 5 mg/kg = 310 mg

Step 2: Check against the maximum single dose
• 310 mg is below 500 mg maximum, so no cap

Step 3: Apply renal dose adjustment
• eGFR 42 falls in 30–60 range: reduce to 60%
• Adjusted dose = 310 mg × 0.60 = 186 mg

Step 4: Calculate the infusion rate
• Diluted to 500 mL, infused over 60 minutes (1 hour)
• Rate = 500 mL ÷ 1 hour = 500 mL/hour

Answer: 500 mL/hour

Why other answers are wrong:
• 450 mL/hour — Calculation error
• 750 mL/hour — Calculation error
• 125 mL/hour — Would imply a 4-hour infusion
• 250 mL/hour — Would imply a 2-hour infusion
summary: Dose = 0.1 mg/kg × 80 kg = 8 mg. Volume = 8 mg ÷ 1 mg/mL = 8 mL.
key_points: Dose: 0.1 mg/kg × 80 kg = 8 mg | Concentration: 1 mg/mL (so mg = mL) | Volume: 8 mL
clinical_pearl: Adenosine must be given as rapid IV push followed by saline flush due to ultra-short half-life (~10 seconds). Warn patient of transient symptoms.
exam_tip: When concentration is 1 mg/mL, the dose in mg equals volume in mL.
related_topics: Adenosine | SVT | Emergency medicines | Bolus dosing

question_text: A 100 kg patient requires a drug with the following parameters: Vd = 1.0 L/kg, target Css = 15 mg/L, half-life = 24 hours, oral bioavailability = 70%. Calculate the clearance using CL = 0.693 × Vd / t½.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the volume of distribution
• Vd = Vd per kg × Weight = 1.0 L/kg × 100 kg = 100 L

Step 2: Calculate the clearance
• CL = 0.693 × Vd ÷ t½
• CL = 0.693 × 100 L ÷ 24 hours
• CL = 69.3 ÷ 24 = 2.8875 ≈ 2.89 L/hour

Answer: 2.89 L/hour

Why other answers are wrong:
• 1.45 L/hour — Half the correct answer
• 0.96 L/hour — May have used Vd of 0.33 L/kg
• 8.67 L/hour — Triple the correct answer
• 1.93 L/hour — Calculation error
summary: Oral:IV ratio is 2:1, meaning oral bioavailability is 50%. Oral dose = 40mg ÷ 0.5 = 80mg.
key_points: IV furosemide = 100% bioavailability | Oral furosemide = ~50% bioavailability | Oral:IV ratio 2:1 means need 2× oral dose | 40mg IV = 80mg oral to achieve same effect
why_wrong_a: 40 mg oral would only deliver ~20mg effective dose (underdosing)
why_wrong_b: 100 mg would be 2.5× the IV dose (overdosing)
why_wrong_d: 60 mg would give ~30mg effective dose (still underdosing)
why_wrong_e: 120 mg would be 3× the IV dose (significant overdose)
clinical_pearl: Furosemide oral bioavailability varies (40-80%). When switching IV to oral, double the dose. Bumetanide has more predictable oral absorption (~80%), making it useful when consistent response is needed.
exam_tip: For bioavailability conversions: Oral dose = IV dose ÷ Bioavailability. If bioavailability is 50% (0.5), oral dose = IV dose × 2.
related_topics: Bioavailability | IV to oral conversion | Loop diuretics | Dose equivalence

question_text: A 70 kg patient with an eGFR of 35 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed aciclovir at 10 mg/kg every 8 hours. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 800 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 25-50 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 50% of normal dose. Aciclovir injection is available as 25 mg/mL in 20 mL vials. What volume of aciclovir injection (25 mg/mL) should be drawn up?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the normal dose
• Normal dose = 70 kg × 10 mg/kg = 700 mg

Step 2: Check against the maximum
• 700 mg is below 800 mg max, so no cap

Step 3: Apply renal dose adjustment
• eGFR 35 falls in 25–50 range: reduce to 50%
• Adjusted dose = 700 mg × 0.50 = 350 mg

Step 4: Calculate volume
• Concentration = 25 mg/mL
• Volume = 350 mg ÷ 25 mg/mL = 14 mL

Answer: 14 mL

Why other answers are wrong:
• 12.6 mL — Calculation error
• 4.66 mL — Significant underdose
• 42 mL — No renal adjustment (700 ÷ 25 = 28, not this either)
• 15.4 mL — Calculation error
summary: Prednisolone 30 mg = Dexamethasone 4.5 mg. Using ratio: 5 mg pred = 0.75 mg dex, so 30 ÷ 5 × 0.75 = 4.5 mg.
key_points: Given equivalence: Prednisolone 5 mg = Dexamethasone 0.75 mg | Calculate number of 5 mg units: 30 ÷ 5 = 6 units | Multiply by dexamethasone equivalent: 6 × 0.75 = 4.5 mg | Dexamethasone is approximately 6-7× more potent than prednisolone
why_wrong_a: 3 mg: Uses incorrect conversion factor - too low
why_wrong_c: 6 mg: Simply divides by 5 without applying the 0.75 factor
why_wrong_d: 7.5 mg: May have used wrong ratio or doubled incorrectly
why_wrong_e: 10 mg: Significantly overestimates - potentially harmful dose
clinical_pearl: Corticosteroid conversion ratios are essential for switching between agents. Remember relative potencies: dexamethasone > betamethasone > methylprednisolone > prednisolone > hydrocortisone.
exam_tip: For steroid conversions: divide dose by 'old' equivalent, multiply by 'new' equivalent. Always verify the direction of conversion.
related_topics: Corticosteroid equivalence | Steroid switching | Glucocorticoid potency

question_text: A patient (weight 58 kg, height 170 cm, BSA 1.41 m²) is prescribed methotrexate (high-dose) at 3000 mg/m² as part of a chemotherapy regimen. The BNF maximum single dose is 8000 mg. The calculated dose is to be diluted in 500 mL of appropriate diluent and infused over 24 hour(s). Methotrexate (high-dose) is available as 5000 mg vials. How many 5000 mg vials are needed?
explanation: Level of 0.4 mmol/L is below therapeutic range (0.6-0.8). A dose increase of ~50% from 800 mg to 1200 mg daily (600 mg BD) would be appropriate.
summary: Level 0.4 mmol/L is below target (0.6-0.8). Increase dose proportionally. 400 mg BD with level 0.4 → 600 mg BD should achieve ~0.6 mmol/L.
key_points: Current: 400 mg BD, level 0.4 mmol/L | Target: 0.6-0.8 mmol/L | Increase by ~50% to reach target | New dose: 600 mg BD
clinical_pearl: Lithium has linear kinetics at therapeutic doses. Level changes proportionally with dose. Recheck level 5-7 days after change.
exam_tip: Proportional adjustment: to increase level from 0.4 to 0.6 (1.5×), increase dose by ~1.5×.
related_topics: Lithium | TDM | Dose adjustment | Bipolar disorder

question_text: A patient requires 40 mmol of potassium chloride added to 500 mL sodium chloride 0.9% to be infused over 4 hours. KCl is available as a 15% solution (1 mmol KCl = 74.5 mg). The maximum peripheral infusion rate is 10 mmol/hour. Calculate the infusion rate in mL/hour.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the infusion rate
• Total volume = 500 mL
• Infusion time = 4 hours
• Infusion rate = 500 mL ÷ 4 hours = 125 mL/hour

Step 2: Check the potassium delivery rate is safe
• Total KCl = 40 mmol over 4 hours = 10 mmol/hour
• Maximum peripheral rate is 10 mmol/hour — at the limit

Answer: 125 mL/hour

Why other answers are wrong:
• 375 mL/hour — Would complete in approximately 80 minutes
• 31.25 mL/hour — Would take 16 hours
• 62.5 mL/hour — Would take 8 hours
• 83.38 mL/hour — Calculation error
summary: Paracetamol 1000 mg QDS = 4000 mg = 4 g daily. Calculation: 1000 mg × 4 = 4000 mg = 4 g.
key_points: QDS = four times daily | Total daily dose: 1000 mg × 4 = 4000 mg | Convert to grams: 4000 ÷ 1000 = 4 g | This is the maximum recommended adult daily dose
why_wrong_a: 3 g would be 750 mg QDS - below the stated dose
why_wrong_b: 6 g would be 1500 mg QDS - dangerous overdose territory
why_wrong_c: 2 g would be 500 mg QDS - half the stated dose
why_wrong_d: 5 g would be 1250 mg QDS - exceeds maximum safe dose
clinical_pearl: 4 g/day is the maximum paracetamol dose for healthy adults. Reduce to 3 g/day in elderly, low body weight (<50 kg), chronic alcohol use, or hepatic impairment. Hepatotoxicity risk increases above recommended doses.
exam_tip: Total daily dose = Single dose × Frequency. QDS=4, TDS=3, BD=2. Always verify total doesn't exceed maximum.
related_topics: Paracetamol dosing | Maximum daily doses | Hepatotoxicity risk

question_text: A 72 kg patient with an eGFR of 25 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed aciclovir at 10 mg/kg every 8 hours. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 800 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 25-50 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 50% of normal dose. Aciclovir injection is available as 25 mg/mL in 20 mL vials. What is the correct aciclovir dose for this patient?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the normal dose
• Normal dose = 72 kg × 10 mg/kg = 720 mg

Step 2: Check against maximum
• 720 mg is below 800 mg max, so no cap

Step 3: Apply renal dose adjustment
• eGFR 25 falls in 25–50 range: reduce to 50%
• Adjusted dose = 720 mg × 0.50 = 360 mg

Answer: 360 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 90 mg — Quarter of correct answer
• 540 mg — May have applied 75% instead of 50%
• 1080 mg — 1.5× the unadjusted dose
• 119.9 mg — Calculation error
summary: Dose = 5 mg/kg × 70 kg = 350 mg.
key_points: Dose: 5 mg/kg | Weight: 70 kg | Total: 5 × 70 = 350 mg
clinical_pearl: Gentamicin 5-7 mg/kg once daily is standard extended-interval dosing. Use ideal body weight for obese patients.
exam_tip: Simple multiplication: mg/kg × weight = total dose.
related_topics: Aminoglycosides | Weight-based dosing

question_text: A patient is started on warfarin for AF — loading regimen using the following titration schedule: 10 mg on day 1 for 1 days; 10 mg on day 2 for 1 days; 5 mg on day 3 for 1 days; check INR, then variable 3-5 mg daily for 25 days for 25 days. Warfarin is available in appropriate tablet strengths. Tablets come in packs of 28. How many packs should be dispensed for the primary drug?
explanation: Simple course calculation: Aspirin 75 mg once daily for cardiovascular prophylaxis. Tablets needed = Doses per day × Duration = 1 × 28 = 28 tablets. Low-dose aspirin (75 mg) inhibits platelet aggregation. Standard pack sizes are often 28 or 56 tablets, aligned with 4- or 8-week supplies.
summary: Tablets = Daily dose × Days = 1 tablet × 28 days = 28 tablets.
key_points: Dose: 75 mg (1 tablet) daily | Duration: 28 days | Quantity: 1 × 28 = 28 tablets
clinical_pearl: Low-dose aspirin (75-81 mg) for cardiovascular prevention. Dispense exact quantities for defined courses.
exam_tip: Tablets per day × number of days = total to dispense.
related_topics: Quantity to dispense | Prescription calculations

question_text: A 68 kg patient is receiving TPN containing 50% glucose (500 mL) and 15% amino acids (500 mL), infused over 12 hours. The maximum recommended glucose delivery is 5 g/kg/day. Calculate the glucose delivery rate in g/kg/day and determine if it is within limits.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total glucose
• 50% glucose = 0.5 g/mL
• Total glucose = 0.5 g/mL × 500 mL = 250 g

Step 2: Calculate the glucose delivery rate
• Rate = Total glucose ÷ Weight = 250 g ÷ 68 kg = 3.68 g/kg/day

Step 3: Check against maximum
• Maximum = 5 g/kg/day
• 3.68 g/kg/day is within limits

Answer: 3.68 g/kg/day (within maximum)

Why other answers are wrong:
• 4.05 g/kg/day — Calculation error
• 0.92 g/kg/day — Quarter of correct answer
• 1.23 g/kg/day — One-third of correct answer
• 0.37 g/kg/day — Decimal error (÷ 10)
summary: Ibuprofen QDS: 400 mg × 4 = 1600 mg daily
key_points: QDS = four times daily | 400 × 4 = 1600 mg | This is the maximum recommended daily dose | Lower doses (1200 mg/day) for OTC use
why_wrong_b: 2400 mg exceeds maximum (used historically but risky)
why_wrong_d: 3200 mg would be dangerous - double the max
why_wrong_e: 1200 mg would be TDS dosing
clinical_pearl: Ibuprofen max 1600 mg/day prescription, 1200 mg/day OTC. Take with or after food. Avoid in renal impairment, heart failure, and asthma (NSAIDs can trigger bronchospasm).
exam_tip: QDS = ×4. Ibuprofen max 1600 mg/day (Rx), 1200 mg/day (OTC). Know NSAID cautions.
related_topics: NSAIDs | Maximum doses | OTC limits | GI protection

question_text: A 82 kg patient in the ICU requires dobutamine at 2.5 micrograms/kg/minute. The infusion is prepared as 250 mg in 250 mL (concentration: 1 mg/mL). The maximum recommended rate is 20 micrograms/kg/minute. Calculate the infusion rate in mL/hour.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the dose per minute
• Dose = 82 kg × 2.5 mcg/kg/min = 205 mcg/min

Step 2: Convert to mg per hour
• 205 mcg/min × 60 = 12300 mcg/hour = 12.3 mg/hour

Step 3: Calculate the infusion rate
• Concentration = 1 mg/mL
• Rate = 12.3 mg/hr ÷ 1 mg/mL = 12.3 mL/hour

Step 4: Confirm within maximum
• Max at 20 mcg/kg/min = 82 × 20 × 60 ÷ 1000 = 98.4 mL/hr
• 12.3 is within maximum

Answer: 12.3 mL/hour

Why other answers are wrong:
• 49.2 mL/hour — Quadruple the correct answer
• 6.15 mL/hour — Half the correct answer
• 4.1 mL/hour — One-third of correct answer
• 1.23 mL/hour — Decimal error (÷ 10)
summary: Calvert formula: Dose = 5 × (60 + 25) = 425 mg
key_points: Calvert formula: Dose = AUC × (GFR + 25) | Dose = 5 × (60 + 25) = 5 × 85 | = 425 mg carboplatin | GFR-adjusted dosing reduces toxicity
clinical_pearl: Carboplatin is uniquely dosed by AUC (area under curve) using Calvert formula, not BSA. GFR should be measured (Cr-EDTA) or calculated. The +25 represents non-renal clearance.
exam_tip: Carboplatin Calvert: Dose (mg) = Target AUC × (GFR + 25). Unique among chemotherapy agents.
related_topics: Carboplatin | Calvert formula | AUC dosing | Renal function

question_text: A patient is started on mirtazapine for depression with insomnia using the following titration schedule: 15 mg at night for 14 days; 30 mg at night for 14 days; 45 mg at night for 28 days. Mirtazapine is available as 15 mg tablets. Tablets come in packs of 28. How many packs should be dispensed for the primary drug?
explanation: Complete steroid taper duration: To taper from 40 mg to 0 mg at 5 mg/week: Weeks = Starting dose ÷ Weekly reduction = 40 mg ÷ 5 mg/week = 8 weeks. Tapering to zero is appropriate after short courses. Longer courses may require slower final reductions (e.g., 2.5 mg or 1 mg steps) to prevent adrenal insufficiency.
summary: Weeks = Starting dose ÷ Weekly reduction = 40 mg ÷ 5 mg/week = 8 weeks.
key_points: Starting: 40 mg | Reduction: 5 mg per week | Weeks to stop: 40 ÷ 5 = 8 weeks
clinical_pearl: Steroid tapers prevent adrenal insufficiency after prolonged use. Slower tapers for longer courses.
exam_tip: Total dose ÷ reduction per interval = number of intervals.
related_topics: Steroid tapering | Corticosteroids

question_text: A 105 kg adult patient requires IV maintenance fluids at 30 mL/kg/day. The fluid prescribed is NaCl 0.18%/Glucose 4% with 20 mmol/L KCl added. NaCl 0.18%/Glucose 4% contains 31 mmol/L sodium. Calculate the infusion rate in mL/hour.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total daily fluid volume
• Volume = Weight × mL/kg/day = 105 kg × 30 mL/kg/day = 3150 mL/day

Step 2: Calculate the hourly infusion rate
• Rate = 3150 mL ÷ 24 hours = 131.25 ≈ 131.2 mL/hour

Answer: 131.2 mL/hour

Why other answers are wrong:
• 524.8 mL/hour — Quadruple the correct answer
• 43.69 mL/hour — One-third of correct answer
• 1312 mL/hour — Decimal error (× 10)
• 13.12 mL/hour — Decimal error (÷ 10)
summary: Omeprazole BD: 20 mg × 2 = 40 mg daily
key_points: BD = bis die = twice daily | 20 mg × 2 = 40 mg per day | Used for H. pylori eradication or severe GORD | Standard maintenance is 20 mg OD
why_wrong_a: 80 mg would be QDS dosing or double-strength BD
why_wrong_c: 20 mg is the single dose, not daily total
why_wrong_d: 30 mg - no standard frequency gives this
why_wrong_e: 60 mg would be TDS dosing
clinical_pearl: Omeprazole 20 mg BD is part of H. pylori triple therapy (with 2 antibiotics for 7 days). Long-term high-dose PPI use increases fracture risk and hypomagnesaemia.
exam_tip: BD = ×2, TDS = ×3, QDS = ×4. Multiply single dose by frequency to get daily dose.
related_topics: Proton pump inhibitors | H. pylori eradication | GORD management | Latin abbreviations

question_text: A patient is started on donepezil for Alzheimer's disease using the following titration schedule: 5 mg once daily for 28 days; 10 mg once daily for 56 days. Donepezil is available as 5 mg tablets. Calculate the total milligrams of donepezil over the entire course.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total mg for each phase
• Phase 1: 5 mg/day × 28 days = 140 mg
• Phase 2: 10 mg/day × 56 days = 560 mg

Step 2: Calculate the total
• Total = 140 + 560 = 700 mg

Answer: 700 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 630 mg — May have used 7 days less
• 2800 mg — Quadruple the correct answer
• 70 mg — Decimal error (÷ 10)
• 7000 mg — Decimal error (× 10)
summary: Tablets = 1/day × 30 days = 30 tablets.
key_points: Dose: 50 mg (1 tablet) once daily | Duration: 30 days | Quantity: 30 tablets
clinical_pearl: Monthly supplies are standard for chronic medications. Consider compliance aids for multiple medications.
exam_tip: Tablets per day × days = quantity to dispense.
related_topics: Quantity to dispense | Prescription calculations

question_text: A 80 kg palliative care patient is currently taking oxycodone 5 mg orally 6 times daily. The patient can no longer swallow and needs to be converted to IV morphine. The morphine will be given via a 24-hour syringe driver. Calculate the total 24-hour dose of IV morphine.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate total daily oral oxycodone
• Oxycodone 5 mg × 6 doses/day = 30 mg oral oxycodone per day

Step 2: Convert oral oxycodone to oral morphine equivalent
• Oral oxycodone is approximately 1.5× the potency of oral morphine
• Oral morphine equivalent = 30 mg × 1.5 = 45 mg oral morphine per day

Step 3: Convert oral morphine to IV morphine
• Oral to IV morphine ratio = 3:1
• IV morphine = 45 mg ÷ 3 = 15 mg ≈ 14.99 mg per 24 hours

Answer: 14.99 mg/24 hours via syringe driver

Why other answers are wrong:
• 4.99 mg/24 hours — Significant underdose
• 10 mg/24 hours — May have used incorrect conversion ratios
• 44.97 mg/24 hours — Triple the correct answer
• 16.49 mg/24 hours — Calculation error
summary: Morphine q4h PRN max: 24 hours ÷ 4 hours = 6 doses × 10 mg = 60 mg/day
key_points: q4h = every 4 hours = up to 6 doses in 24 hours | 24 ÷ 4 = 6 maximum doses | 6 × 10 mg = 60 mg maximum daily | PRN = as needed, not scheduled
why_wrong_a: 120 mg would be 12 doses (q2h) - too frequent
why_wrong_b: 90 mg would be 9 doses - doesn't fit q4h pattern
why_wrong_d: 30 mg would only be 3 doses in 24 hours
why_wrong_e: 45 mg would be 4.5 doses - not possible
clinical_pearl: Morphine is a Schedule 2 controlled drug. Track total daily opioid intake for safety. If regularly using maximum PRN doses, consider scheduled analgesia with PRN for breakthrough.
exam_tip: Maximum doses per day: q4h = 6, q6h = 4, q8h = 3, q12h = 2. Divide 24 by the interval.
related_topics: Opioid dosing | Controlled drugs | PRN medications | Pain management

question_text: A 68 kg patient with an eGFR of 50 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed gentamicin at 5 mg/kg once daily. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 500 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 30-60 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 60% of normal dose. Gentamicin injection is available as 40 mg/mL in 2 mL vials. What is the correct gentamicin dose for this patient?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the normal dose
• Normal dose = 68 kg × 5 mg/kg = 340 mg

Step 2: Check against maximum
• 340 mg is below 500 mg max, so no cap

Step 3: Apply renal dose adjustment
• eGFR 50 falls in 30–60 range: reduce to 60%
• Adjusted dose = 340 mg × 0.60 = 204 mg

Answer: 204 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 183.6 mg — Calculation error
• 51 mg — Quarter of correct answer
• 408 mg — Double (no renal adjustment divided by 2 incorrectly)
• 102 mg — Half the correct answer
summary: Daily dose = Weight × mg/kg/day = 20 kg × 25 mg/kg/day = 500 mg/day.
key_points: Weight: 20 kg | Dose: 25 mg/kg/day | Daily dose = 20 × 25 = 500 mg/day | May be divided into 2-3 doses
why_wrong_e: 250 mg = 12.5 mg/kg/day
clinical_pearl: Amoxicillin 25mg/kg/day is standard for mild infections. Higher doses (80-90mg/kg/day) used for otitis media or suspected resistant pneumococci. Divide into 2-3 doses for consistent levels.
exam_tip: Daily dose = weight × mg/kg/DAY. This gives total for the day, which is then divided by frequency.
related_topics: Paediatric antibiotics | Weight-based dosing | Amoxicillin | Daily dose calculations

question_text: A patient is started on quetiapine for bipolar depression using the following titration schedule: 50 mg at night for 3 days; 100 mg at night for 4 days; 200 mg at night for 7 days; 300 mg at night for 42 days. Quetiapine is available as 25 mg tablets. Calculate the total milligrams of quetiapine over the entire course.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total mg for each phase
• Phase 1: 50 mg/day × 3 days = 150 mg
• Phase 2: 100 mg/day × 4 days = 400 mg
• Phase 3: 200 mg/day × 7 days = 1400 mg
• Phase 4: 300 mg/day × 42 days = 12600 mg

Step 2: Calculate the total
• Total = 150 + 400 + 1400 + 12600 = 14550 mg

Answer: 14550 mg

Why other answers are wrong:
• 58200 mg — Quadruple the correct answer
• 4845.1 mg — One-third of correct answer
• 43650 mg — Triple the correct answer
• 145500 mg — Decimal error (× 10)
summary: Lansoprazole supply: 1 capsule OD × 28 days = 28 capsules
key_points: OD = once daily | 28 × 1 = 28 capsules | Standard 4-week PPI course | Review needed after 4-8 weeks
why_wrong_b: 56 capsules would be 8 weeks or BD
why_wrong_e: 21 capsules would be 3 weeks
clinical_pearl: PPIs should be reviewed after 4-8 weeks. Long-term use risks include hypomagnesaemia, C. difficile infection, B12 deficiency, and osteoporotic fractures. Step down to lowest effective dose or H2 blocker.
exam_tip: OD for 28 days = 28 capsules. PPIs: take before food, review regularly for deprescribing.
related_topics: Proton pump inhibitors | GORD treatment | PPI deprescribing | Long-term PPI risks

question_text: A 80 kg patient with an eGFR of 55 mL/min/1.73m² is prescribed vancomycin at 15 mg/kg every 12 hours. The BNF recommends a maximum single dose of 2000 mg and the following renal dose adjustments: eGFR 30-60 mL/min/1.73m²: reduce to 75% of normal dose. Vancomycin injection is available as 50 mg/mL in 10 mL vials. What volume of vancomycin injection (50 mg/mL) should be drawn up?
explanation:
**Pharmaceutical Calculation**

Using the standard calculation method for this type of problem:

**Answer: 180 mg**

**Method:** Apply the appropriate formula based on the given values, ensuring correct unit conversions throughout the calculation.

**Clinical note:** Always double-check calculations, especially for high-risk medications.
summary: 30mg every 4 hours PRN. Maximum doses in 24hrs = 24÷4 = 6 doses. Maximum = 6 × 30mg = 180mg.
key_points: Dose: 30 mg per administration | Frequency: every 4 hours (q4h) as needed | Maximum doses in 24h = 24 ÷ 4 = 6 doses | Maximum daily = 6 × 30 = 180 mg
why_wrong_b: 90 mg = only 3 doses (every 8 hours, not q4h)
why_wrong_c: 270 mg = 9 doses (more than possible in 24h at q4h)
why_wrong_e: 135 mg = 4.5 doses (not a whole number of q4h intervals)
clinical_pearl: Codeine max daily dose is typically 240mg, but this q4h schedule gives 180mg. PRN (as needed) means patient may take less, but this calculates the maximum possible if taken at every interval.
exam_tip: For PRN max daily: divide 24 hours by dosing interval to get max doses, then multiply by dose. q4h = 6 doses, q6h = 4 doses, q8h = 3 doses.

question_text: A patient (weight 98 kg, height 150 cm, BSA 1.88 m²) is prescribed cyclophosphamide at 600 mg/m² as part of a chemotherapy regimen. The BNF maximum single dose is 1500 mg. The calculated dose is to be diluted in 250 mL of appropriate diluent and infused over 1 hour(s). Calculate the infusion rate in mL/hour.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the dose using BSA
• Dose = 600 mg/m² × 1.88 m² = 1128 mg

Step 2: Check against maximum
• 1128 mg is below 1500 mg max, so no cap

Step 3: Calculate infusion rate
• Diluted in 250 mL, infused over 1 hour
• Rate = 250 mL ÷ 1 hour = 250 mL/hour

Answer: 250 mL/hour

Why other answers are wrong:
• 275 mL/hour — Calculation error
• 25 mL/hour — Decimal error (÷ 10)
• 83.25 mL/hour — Would imply a 3-hour infusion
• 1000 mL/hour — Would imply a 15-minute infusion
summary: BSA dosing: 1.7 m² × 100 mg/m² = 170 mg
key_points: Dose = BSA × dose per m² | 1.7 × 100 = 170 mg | BSA accounts for body size | Average adult BSA ≈ 1.7-1.9 m²
why_wrong_e: 340 mg would be 3.4 m² (impossibly large)
clinical_pearl: BSA dosing is standard for chemotherapy as toxicity correlates with drug exposure. Mosteller formula: BSA = √[(height cm × weight kg) ÷ 3600]. Always verify BSA before each cycle.
exam_tip: BSA dosing: multiply BSA (m²) by dose (mg/m²). Check BSA is realistic (adults typically 1.5-2.2 m²).
related_topics: BSA calculation | Chemotherapy dosing | Mosteller formula | Body surface area

question_text: A patient is started on spironolactone for heart failure (initiation) using the following titration schedule: 25 mg daily for 28 days; 50 mg daily for 28 days. Spironolactone is available as 25 mg tablets. Tablets come in packs of 28. How many packs should be dispensed for the primary drug?
explanation: Two-month supply calculation: OD (once daily) for 56 days (8 weeks). Tablets = 1/day × 56 days = 56 tablets. Amlodipine is a calcium channel blocker for hypertension; 5 mg is the usual starting dose. 56-day supplies reduce pharmacy visits for stable chronic conditions.
summary: Tablets = 1/day × 56 days = 56 tablets.
key_points: Dose: 5 mg (1 tablet) once daily | Duration: 56 days | Quantity: 56 tablets
clinical_pearl: 8-week supplies common for stable chronic conditions. Matches typical review intervals.
exam_tip: Tablets per day × days = quantity to dispense.
